coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots as fluorescence sensors for label-free detection of folic acid in biological samples
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Wenyi
Wu, Bingbing
Li, Zhongfeng
Wang, Yingfeng
Zhou, Jing
Li, Yaping
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been fabricated by a facile single-step pyrolysis method from citric acid and ethylene imine polymer. When excited at 359 nm, CQDs show intense blue fluorescence at 434 nm. The fluorescence can be effectively quenched by folic acid (FA), which is attributed to the combination of static quenching and inner filter effect. Thus, the CQDs are developed as an efficient fluorescent sensing platform for label-free sensitive and selective detection of FA. Key parameters influencing the detection were investigated, such as incubation time, salt concentration, selectivity and potential interferences. Under the optimal conditions, a good linearity was observed for the emission intensity against 1.14–47.57 μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.99. The limit of detection was found to be 0.38 μM. The practical application of the sensing system was demonstrated by analyzing FA in human urine samples. The sample recoveries fell in the range of 82.0%–113.1% with RSDs ≤ 10.9%, which presented its reliable and feasible application in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescent sensor
Folic acid
Inner filter effect
Static quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Quantitative detection of folic acid in human urine samples
Determination of folic acid in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The selectivity of the CQDs for FA detection in the presence of various interfering substances at λex = 359 nm. Inset: Photograph of the CQDs containing various potential interferences under 365 nm UV light. (B) The fluorescence changes of CQDs in the present of FA toward the potential interfering substances at the same concentration.

(A) Fluorescent responses of CQDs upon the addition of 0–47.57 μM FA. Inset: Photograph of the CQDs solution (left) and CQDs with FA solution (right) under 365 nm UV light; (B) The linear relationship between F0/F and the concentration of FA. F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity at 434 nm in the absence and presence of FA, respectively.

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of FA, CQDs, and mixture of FA and CQDs in methanol solution, respectively; (B) Normalized excitation (emission at 434 nm) and emission (excitation at 359 nm) spectra of CQDs in methanol solution and UV–Vis absorption spectrum of FA in methanol solution.
Schematic illustration of FA detection mechanism using CQDs.
Analytical performance of the proposed method in determination of FA in human urine samples.
Lifetime data analysis of CQDs in absence and presence of FA recorded in methanol, λex = 359 nm.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots as fluorescence sensors for label-free detection of folic acid in biological samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Capital Normal University, Beijing, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Capital Normal University, Beijing, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, Capital Normal University, 100048 Beijing, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Wenyi Zhang and Bingbing Wu contributed equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been fabricated by a facile single-step pyrolysis method from citric acid and ethylene imine polymer. When excited at 359 nm, CQDs show intense blue fluorescence at 434 nm. The fluorescence can be effectively quenched by folic acid (FA), which is attributed to the combination of static quenching and inner filter effect. Thus, the CQDs are developed as an efficient fluorescent sensing platform for label-free sensitive and selective detection of FA. Key parameters influencing the detection were investigated, such as incubation time, salt concentration, selectivity and potential interferences. Under the optimal conditions, a good linearity was observed for the emission intensity against 1.14–47.57 μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.99. The limit of detection was found to be 0.38 μM. The practical application of the sensing system was demonstrated by analyzing FA in human urine samples. The sample recoveries fell in the range of 82.0%–113.1% with RSDs ≤ 10.9%, which presented its reliable and feasible application in real samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Folic acid (FA) is one of the most important vitamins in human body. It participates in several vital metabolic processes, such as synthesis of S-adenosylmethionine (a methyl group donor in several methylation reactions) [1], synthesis of purines and thymidylate [2], as well as histidine catabolism and serine interconversion [3]. Lack of FA may cause anemia associated with leukopenia. Lack of FA for pregnant women may lead to cleft lip, heart defect and low birth weight of the fetus [4]. Furthermore, FA deficiency is also related to Alzheimer, senile dementia and autism [5]. However, excess FA may cause zinc deficiency, anorexia, nausea, and a series of gastrointestinal syndrome [4]. Therefore, there is a high demand for quantitative analysis of FA in biological samples. In response to this need, various methods have been developed for FA analysis, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6–11], flow-injection chemiluminescence (CL) [12,13], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays [14], electrochemistry [15] and so on. However, among these methods, due to the requirement of expensive equipment, long consuming time, complicated pretreatments, poor selectivity, as well as failure applications in complex biological samples, development of a novel approach for FA analysis is still far from completion.

Quantum dots (QDs), semiconductor nanocrystals, have played an important role in designing chemical sensors attributed to their high optical quantum yields and photo-stability [16–22]. But their biotoxicity, environmental concerns as well as high costs are the bottlenecks in their practical applications [23,24]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new class of carbon nanomaterials, have emerged as potential competitors to conventional semiconductor quantum dots and sparked enormous research interests recently. They possess superior properties, such as easy synthesis, low cost, chemical inertness, tunable photoluminescence and excellent biocompatibility [25], which provoke their potential applications in chemical sensing [26,27], biosensing [28,29], bioimaging [30] and photocatalysis [31].

Herein, a facile and label-free fluorescent sensing system for FA analysis is developed based on CQDs without further functionalization. CQDs are synthesized via a facile one-step pyrolysis method. There are two significant factors behind detection of FA: one factor is formation of a complex between CQDs and FA; the other is the overlap between the excitation band of CQDs and the absorption band of FA. The fluorescence intensity of CQDs shows a sensitive and selective response to FA at the concentration ranging from 1.14 to 47.57 μM with a detection limit of 0.38 μM. Furthermore, the presented strategy is demonstrated a great potential application in FA analysis in human urine.

Citric acid (CA) was purchased from Alfa Aesar (Ward Hill, MA, USA). Ethylene imine polymer (EIP) with molecular weight of 600 and column-layer chromatographic silica gel were from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Hydrochloric acid and methanol were obtained from Beijing Chemical Factory (Beijing, China). Dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 500 Da) was purchased from MYM Biological Technology Company Limited (Beijing, China). NaCl, CaCl2, BaCl2, KCl, MgCl2, NaNO3, ascorbic acid, glucose, tryptophan, phenylalanine and urea were from Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany). Ultrapure water was obtained from Millipore Simplicity system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA). Fresh human urine samples were collected from three healthy adult volunteers.

UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were measured by using an F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The fluorescence lifetime was obtained by using a FLS920 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd., UK).

The CQDs were synthesized by pyrolysis of CA in the presence of EIP. The synthesis process was similar to the previous work [32]. Briefly, 1.0 g CA and 0.5 g EIP were dissolved in 10 mL hot water and heated below 200 °C using an oil bath. When most of the water was evaporated and pale-yellow gel was formed, 1 mL water was added and the mixture was kept heating. The above procedure was repeated until the gel color became orange. The product was dissolved in 10 mL water and purified by silica gel column chromatography using 0.01 M HCl solution as the mobile phase. Then the obtained solution was dialyzed to remove hydrochloric acid by using dialysis bag (MWCO: 500 Da), vacuum-evaporated in a rotary evaporator at 60 °C, diluted to 0.03 g/mL aqueous solution and stored at 4 °C before use.

The detection of FA was carried out as follows. 200 μL 0.2 mg/mL CQDs aqueous solution was added into 6 mL FA of methanol solution with different concentrations. The mixture was incubated in the air-bath shaker at 25 °C with the speed of 230 r/min for 9 h. The fluorescence spectra of mixture solution were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 359 nm. The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of CQDs and the concentration of FA was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative detection of folic acid in human urine samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practicability of the assay in real samples, human urine samples collected from three healthy volunteers were investigated. For the urine samples analysis, urine samples (5 mL) were firstly centrifuged at 12,000 r/min for 10 min to remove undissolved impurity. Then the supernatant was collected and dried by vacuum freezing drying. Pale yellow powder was obtained and re-dissolved in 5 mL methanol by ultrasound, followed by centrifugation to remove undissolved residue. Finally, the obtained supernatant was diluted 1000 fold and mixed with CQDs aqueous solution under the same experimental conditions illustrated in Section 2.4. Fluorescence intensity was recorded at the excitation wavelength of 359 nm, and the standard recoveries were calculated by standard addition method.

For FA analysis, incubation time is a key parameter to be optimized, which has a great influence on the method accuracy. The fluorescence intensity of the mixture was measured at a finite time interval and the recorded data is displayed in Fig. S1. The fluorescence intensity decreased obviously when the mixture was incubated in the beginning. When the incubation time was prolonged to 6 h, the fluorescence intensity was stable. Under the comprehensive consideration of stability and time cost, 9 h was selected as the optimal incubation time in the FA sensing system for further study.

In order to confirm the feasibility of the assay for FA analysis in biological samples, the effect of salt concentration on fluorescence intensity of CQDs in the presence of FA was investigated in detail. As illustrated in Fig. S2, with the increase in the concentration of NaCl, fluorescence intensity of the mixture remained stable and no obvious signal changes occurred even if the concentration of NaCl increased to 0.18 M. The result confirms that our assay has a perfect salt-resistant ability and facilitates its application in biological samples.

To figure out whether our system was specific for detection of FA, the selectivity and competition experiments were carried out by adding various biological interfering substances (cations, anions, amino acids as well as other small molecules) into the CQDs solution in the absence and presence of FA and by detecting the change of the fluorescence intensity. Fig. 1A (inset) depicts the photograph of CQDs solution with addition of various interfering substances under UV light. It can be seen obviously that the fluorescence intensity of CQDs was effectively quenched by FA, while others still maintained upon the addition of the potential interferences (Fig. 1A). Moreover, the above-mentioned potential interfering substances were also added into the CQDs solution in the presence of FA respectively, and then the fluorescence intensities were recorded. As displayed in Fig. 1B, the influence of the coexisting substances on the fluorescence intensity of FA-CQDs system is negligible. These observations indicate that the CQDs show a high selectivity toward FA over others, which demonstrates that the proposed method for FA analysis has a good anti-jamming capability.

The analytical performance of the proposed method was studied under the optimal conditions. As displayed in Fig. 2 , with the increase in the concentration of FA, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs at 434 nm decreases gradually. A good linear relationship (r2 = 0.99) between F0/F and the concentration of FA is obtained in the range from 1.14 μM to 47.57 μM. The regression equation is: F0/F = 1.5 × 104 CFA (C: M) + 1.00 and the limit of detection calculated as signal-to-noise ratio of 3 is 0.38 μM. F0 and F corresponds to the fluorescence intensity of CQDs in the absence and presence of FA, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of folic acid in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the practicability of the proposed method, FA in human urine samples was detected. As shown in Table 1 , the recoveries range from 82.0% to 113.1% via adding three concentration levels of FA in real urine samples, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) of three repeated detections for each sample are no more than 10.9%, which indicates that our assay is promising for analyzing FA in real samples.

To figure out the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the fluorescence quenching of CQDs by FA was interpreted by Stern-Volmer analysis. The Stern-Volmer plot exhibits a strong linear dependence on the concentration of FA (Fig. 2B). In addition, with the increase in incubation temperature, the calculated Stern-Volmer quenching constant (KSV) decreases obviously and is 1.51 × 104 M− 1 at 25 °C, 8.08 × 103 M− 1 at 35 °C and 3.46 × 103 M− 1 at 45 °C, respectively (Table S1). This can be explained by the fact that some of bound probe-quencher complexes dissociate and lower percentage of quenching occurs at higher incubation temperature, which indicates the static fluorescence quenching process. This result fairly agrees well with the one obtained by UV–Vis. As shown in Fig. 3A, the perturbation of UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CQDs occurs after addition of FA, which indicates the formation of the complex between CQDs and FA.

As static quenching process occurs by forming probe-quencher complexes in the CQDs-FA system, the number of binding sites (n) is calculated from the regression curve based on the following equation:
(1)
log
F 0
− F
F
= log
K a
+ nlog
Q
where F0 and F is the fluorescence intensity of CQDs in the absence and presence of the quencher (i.e. FA) in the system respectively, [Q] is the concentration of the quencher, Ka is the association constant and n is the number of binding sites. From Eq. (1), the number of binding sites (n) in CQDs-FA system was obtained to be 0.89, which could be rounded off to 1.

The measurement of fluorescence lifetimes is another common approach to study the quenching mechanism, thus the nature of static quenching was also studied by time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy. Compared with the change of the fluorescence intensity, the fluorescence lifetimes of CQDs remain almost stable with addition of various concentrations of FA in solution, which confirms that the static quenching takes place (Table 2 ).

Furthermore, Fig. 3B reveals an overlap between the absorption spectrum of FA and excitation spectrum of CQDs. This result implies that inner filter effect (IFE) occurs and is another important factor contributing to the quenching by FA. Therefore, according to quenching mechanism discussed above, the illustration for the interaction between CQDs and FA is presented in Scheme 1 .

In summary, we have demonstrated a novel type of CQDs fluorescent sensing system based on a facile single-step pyrolysis method. Taking advantage of the effective fluorescence quenching induced by the static quenching and inner filter effect, a highly sensitive and selective CQDs-based fluorescent sensor for label-free detection of FA has been constructed. Moreover, the proposed fluorescent sensor exhibits satisfactory performance for analyzing FA in human urine samples. Most importantly, the as-prepared CQDs generate a new research avenue for FA detection and hold a promising application in biological samples.

Wenyi Zhang: Investigation, Visualization. Bingbing Wu: Writing - Original Draft, Formal analysis. Zhongfeng Li: Resources. Yingfeng Wang: Project administration. Jing Zhou: Resources. Yaping Li: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for the support by grants from National Natural Science Foundation of China (21575090) and Scientific Research Project of Beijing Educational Committee (KM201810028008). The authors acknowledge the support from Analysis and Testing of Capital Normal University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2019.117931.
